glossary.definition: It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017) . The structure of the matrix differs a little bit for the binary classification task and multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) .

glossary.definition: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017) . Kubat (2017) gives an example for the dataset containing 100 data objects, 95 of which labelled as belonging to Class 0, and 5 labelled as Class 1. “In this case, a poorly built “dummy” model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy, which indicates a very strong model. However, this model is not really predictive and Accuracy is not the right performance metric to evaluate the power of this model” (Kubat, 2017) . Figure 1 shows the calculation of accuracy for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes. Figure 2 shows the calculation of precision for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.term: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Schemas used to develop rule-based systems, representing a set of problem-solving instructions rather than knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems.

glossary.definition: Rules stored in a knowledge base that are matched against a pattern in each iteration of the control cycle.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base for real-world problems includes a considerable number of production rules.

glossary.definition: A rule-based expert system consisting of a knowledge base, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component of a production system that stores facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures, and other case-specific information.
glossary.sentence: a working memory;

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired when IF statements are added to the THEN part of rules.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired;

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired when the THEN part of rules contains actions.
glossary.sentence: however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: The control cycle performed by the inference engine to link rules in the knowledge base with facts in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle "Recognize-act" (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: Essential components of an expert system that provide the user with the reasoning behind a recommendation or conclusion.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.

glossary.definition: Additional components that may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results

glossary.definition: An interface intended to edit rules in the knowledge base and check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The operational principles of a system where production rules are matched against a pattern, and if a rule fires, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 explains the operational principles of the control cycle “Recognize-act”.

glossary.definition: Methods used to make the search process more effective, including control through the selection of search direction, control through the selection of rule sequence and structure, and control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: A search process that begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.

glossary.definition: A search process where a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution method based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one, also known as the more specific rules strategy.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Systems that use production rules as a natural way of knowledge representation, offering a unified structure, separation of knowledge and control, and modularity of rules.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ); Uniform structure. Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ).

glossary.definition: The characteristic of production rules having no syntactic interactions, supporting incremental development of expert systems by successively adding, deleting, or modifying the knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.

glossary.definition: The ability to trace the execution of production rules and provide meaningful explanations of the system’s current state and action due to the modularity of rules and their iterative execution.
glossary.sentence: The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed.

glossary.definition: A mechanism used by rule-based expert systems to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: Systems that support decision making.
glossary.sentence: Mallach E.G. (1994). Understanding decision support systems and expert systems.

glossary.definition: Systems that exhibit intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Negnevitsky M. (2011). Artificial Intelligence: a Guide to Intelligent Systems.
